title: Smooching and Mooching

jokes.text: Daniel and Nick at a garage sale debating who has better 'crap'
jokes.setup: Two burnouts browsing a stranger's garage sale junk
jokes.punchline: Daniel earnestly insists his own crap is way better than this crap, as though there's a crap hierarchy worth defending
jokes.explanation: Grounded opener that efficiently characterizes Daniel and Nick as lovable, self-unaware slackers. The word 'crap' repeated as a category of value is mildly funny. Not exceptional but does solid table-setting work.

jokes.text: Daniel's theory that people who keep books are just bragging: 'OOH! Look at all these books I've read. I'm so clever!'
jokes.setup: Nick wonders why people save books they've already read
jokes.punchline: Daniel delivers a fully-formed conspiracy theory about book-owning as performative intellectual flex, complete with mockery voice
jokes.explanation: This is perfectly Daniel — a guy who has constructed an entire ideology to justify not reading. The confidence of the delivery and the specificity of the mockery voice elevate it. Strong character integration: only a specific kind of insecure underachiever would resent books this way.

jokes.text: Nick says finding bongos would bring the Andopolis drum kit to 'an even 30 pieces' — Daniel says '31 pieces'
jokes.setup: Nick is excited about bongos at the garage sale
jokes.punchline: The incidental revelation that Nick already has 29-30 pieces in his drum kit — a comically excessive number — punctuated by Daniel's correction implying he has been keeping count
jokes.explanation: Lovely economy here. In one exchange the show tells you Nick is obsessively serious about his drum kit (which his dad will later sell), and Daniel pays close enough attention to correct the count. Both characters illuminated in eight words. The correction is the joke and it lands clean.

jokes.text: Nick's dad is mad because Nick was playing 'American Band' while his dad was watching 60 Minutes
jokes.setup: Nick's dad won't talk to him — Daniel assumes it's about a report card
jokes.punchline: Nick clarifies: he drowned out 60 Minutes with Grand Funk Railroad, which is somehow both more and less reasonable than a bad report card
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '60 Minutes' vs. Grand Funk Railroad is the joke — a generational clash condensed into a title collision. Functional and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Lindsay asks 'Nick... where are your drums?' — the room is visibly empty where a massive kit once stood
jokes.setup: Nick arrives at the Weir house looking shell-shocked
jokes.punchline: Lindsay walks in and asks the obvious question about the conspicuously absent drum kit — the understatement of a question that opens onto a disaster
jokes.explanation: Deadpan visual understatement — the gaping absence where 30+ drums used to be, and Lindsay's mild 'where are your drums?' registers well. Brief but effective.

jokes.text: 'Fine. With a little more effort, you could end up living in a cardboard box.'
jokes.setup: Nick argues that his drums are his future.
jokes.punchline: The father's deadpan projection of failure — not 'you'll never make it' but the specific, visual endpoint of a cardboard box — is bleakly funny in its precision.
jokes.explanation: The humor is in the father's grim specificity. It's not entirely original as parental-doom dialogue goes, but the delivery context (middle of a heated argument) and visual specificity elevate it slightly.

jokes.text: Nick demands money for the sold drums; his dad fires back: 'How much did you spend on drums and how much did you spend on drugs?'
jokes.setup: Nick righteously demands payment for his drums that his dad sold without permission
jokes.punchline: His dad undercuts the moral high ground instantly by implying Nick's drug expenditure probably exceeds his drum investment
jokes.explanation: This line does a lot: it wins the argument, reveals something true about Nick, and has a rhetorical snap to it. The drums/drugs parallel construction is elegant. Dark and earned — we've seen Nick has a drug problem. One of the episode's sharper lines.

jokes.text: Nick storms out: 'I'm outta here, and I hope that you enjoy your peace and quiet'
jokes.setup: Nick's dramatic exit after losing the argument
jokes.punchline: The 'peace and quiet' line backfires as a threat — his dad literally sold the drums to get peace and quiet. Nick has accidentally stated his father's dream outcome as a curse.
jokes.explanation: Nick's obliviousness is the joke. He delivers what he thinks is a devastating parting shot that is in fact his dad's explicit goal. Perfectly character-integrated dramatic irony — the audience sees it even if Nick doesn't.

jokes.text: Cut to the geeks watching The Jerk — 'There's something wrong with these cans! He hates these cans!'
jokes.setup: Transition from Nick's drama to the geeks hanging out
jokes.punchline: They're quoting The Jerk with apparent rapturous delight — the movie's broad gag about a sniper shooting at cans lands as a comedy-within-a-comedy moment, and their joy is infectious
jokes.explanation: Smart cultural embed — The Jerk is a real touchstone for this generation, and the boys quoting it with reverence is character-authentic. The joy in the room elevates a borrowed joke.

jokes.text: The Caddyshack vs. The Jerk vs. Stripes debate — Neal recites the entire second half of Stripes on demand
jokes.setup: The group debates whether Caddyshack is better than The Jerk; someone claims Stripes is uneven and 'you can't tell me what happens in the second half'
jokes.punchline: Neal immediately, fluently, and enthusiastically recites the entire second-half plot of Stripes from memory, proving not only that he has watched it obsessively but that the 'uneven' argument is dead on arrival
jokes.explanation: This is a gem of character comedy. The setup implies no one could possibly remember the second half, then Neal demolishes the premise with total, unselfconscious specificity. It's funny because it reveals how much of Neal's inner life is memorized movie plots — and also because he ends mid-sentence, interrupted, as if he could go on forever. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Cindy pulls Sam away from the group for a private chat — Bill, Neal, and Daniel's confused/excited silent reaction
jokes.setup: Cindy Sanders, the popular girl Sam has had a crush on forever, suddenly wants to talk to him privately
jokes.punchline: The audience sees the geeks silently processing this miracle — their faces doing all the comedy work as Sam is selected by the most desirable girl in school
jokes.explanation: Silent reaction comedy — the geeks watching Sam get pulled away by Cindy would generate a classic comedy-of-recognition beat. Their stunned faces register as pure character comedy.

jokes.text: Sam runs back to the group: 'My God! It's happening!'
jokes.setup: Cindy has just told Sam she'll call him
jokes.punchline: 'It's happening' — Sam's explosion of barely-contained teenage ecstasy is disproportionate and perfectly adolescent
jokes.explanation: Three words doing maximum work. The economy is excellent — 'it's happening' captures years of Sam's longing for Cindy in two syllables. It's a great teen comedy exclamation, completely in character.

jokes.text: Nick asks if anyone wants to give him a neck rub in the cafeteria
jokes.setup: Nick is describing his new homeless status and sore neck from sleeping on Daniel's floor
jokes.punchline: He casually asks for a neck rub at the lunch table — completely oblivious to how this lands socially
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe. Nick's social obliviousness is a consistent character note. The joke is in the casualness of the ask more than any specific twist.

jokes.text: Kim reveals Nick slept on Daniel's infested carpet, then Daniel says 'Better than sleeping in my Maverick'
jokes.setup: Nick is described as crashing on Daniel's floor because his dad kicked him out
jokes.punchline: Daniel clarifies the floor is an upgrade from his car — he has apparently slept in his Maverick enough times to have a reference point
jokes.explanation: Classic deadpan escalation. Daniel's car-sleeping history is revealed with complete nonchalance. Only Daniel Desario would have this comparison available, and only he would offer it as comfort. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Ken refuses to let Nick stay over: 'My dad. He doesn't even let my grandma stay over. He's got issues.'
jokes.setup: Nick desperate for a place to sleep, asks Ken
jokes.punchline: Ken's dad is so inhospitable he bans even his own mother — presented with perfect Ken flatness as just a fact about the man
jokes.explanation: Peak Ken delivery. 'He's got issues' as the endpoint of escalating grandma-banning is perfectly timed. The joke rewards Ken's specific deadpan misanthropy — this is unmistakably a Ken line.

jokes.text: Daniel explains Nick didn't flush the toilet and his mom 'almost had a heart attack' — Nick protests 'He's just joking.' Daniel: 'I'm not joking!'
jokes.setup: Daniel says he can't have Nick over again because of something Nick did
jokes.punchline: Daniel reveals the toilet incident in excruciating social detail as Nick tries to shut him down — Daniel insisting he's not joking while Nick begs him to stop amplifies the cringe
jokes.explanation: Good cringe-comedy escalation — Nick's mortification at having his bathroom habits aired publicly, Daniel's total lack of filter, and the overlapping 'can you not talk about this' create a squirmy but funny moment.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir grousing about 'those religious people bothering me all day' right before Nick shows up at the door
jokes.setup: The family debate is interrupted by a doorbell; Harold assumes it's religious people pestering him
jokes.punchline: It's Nick — which turns out to be, from Harold's perspective, possibly worse
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection — Harold's petty complaint about door-to-door people pivots into Nick's arrival, which Harold must now navigate diplomatically. The irony that Nick ends up being a longer-term intrusion than any Jehovah's Witness is implicit.

jokes.text: Lindsay's dad's debate position on women presidents: 'It's called 3 irrational days per month. Now I would have no issue with the other 27, but we're talking about the atomic bomb here!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks her dad why there can't be a woman president
jokes.punchline: Harold's anti-woman-president argument invokes PMS and nuclear weapons in the same breath — the gap between the scale of his concerns (atomic bomb) and his 'reasoning' (3 days of irrationality) is the joke
jokes.explanation: Harold's casual sexism is played as character comedy — he's not a villain, he's a man of his time and generation, and his 'logic' is presented as absurd. The atomic bomb escalation is the funny part. Lindsay rolling her eyes and Jean's 'Oh, Harold!' are the straight-man reactions.

jokes.text: Lindsay responds: 'You know, men get periods too. It has to do with your body tides.' Harold: 'My body does not have a tide.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay counters Harold's sexist period logic
jokes.punchline: Lindsay invents 'body tides' as a pseudo-scientific framework for male periods; Harold's quiet, dignified 'My body does not have a tide' is the defensive response of a man who has just had his tidal autonomy challenged
jokes.explanation: 'My body does not have a tide.' is an excellent Harold line — his categorical rejection of the body-tide concept is both funny and perfectly Harold. Lindsay's 'body tides' theory is absurdist weaponized nonsense, and Harold taking it seriously enough to deny it makes the joke.

jokes.text: Nick sniffs the air from the doorway: 'Let me guess... Meat?' Harold: 'Congratulations.'
jokes.setup: Nick is rhapsodizing about how good it smells inside the Weir house
jokes.punchline: Nick performs a dramatic sniff and correctly identifies 'meat' as the ingredient — an almost uselessly broad deduction — then Harold's deadpan 'Congratulations' is perfectly withering
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest moments. Nick's theatrical nose-work identifying 'meat' is funny on its own — but Harold's bone-dry 'Congratulations' for the world's most obvious deduction is the real payoff. Perfect Harold Weir characterization: polite contempt delivered with impeccable timing.

jokes.text: Nick accepts the dinner invitation: 'YEAH! That would rock! I haven't eaten dinner yet!'
jokes.setup: Mrs. Weir politely offers Nick dinner; Harold gives a strained 'yeah... it would... rock'
jokes.punchline: Nick's enthusiasm is so naked and desperate it crosses from charming to embarrassing — he blurts that he hasn't eaten as if removing any ambiguity about his neediness
jokes.explanation: Harold being forced to say 'it would... rock' about his daughter's ex-boyfriend crashing dinner is quietly funny. Nick's oblivious joy at a free meal makes it cringe-warm. Running gag: Nick attaches himself to the Weir family like a barnacle.

jokes.text: Nick compliments Mrs. Weir's pot roast: 'You're even a better cook than my mom'
jokes.setup: Nick is laying on the compliments at dinner to ingratiate himself with the Weirs
jokes.punchline: The mom-comparison is a socially awkward over-share — the kind of desperate flattery that reveals just how much Nick needs this family right now
jokes.explanation: Mild. The joke is in Jean's gracious deflection ('You don't get to be that tall without someone doing something right nutritionally') more than Nick's compliment itself. Jean's non-sequitur physical logic is the funnier element.

jokes.text: Jean's response: 'You don't get to be as tall as you are without somebody doing something right nutritionally'
jokes.setup: Nick compliments Jean's cooking and implies his own mother is worse
jokes.punchline: Jean deflects with a nutritional-logic non-sequitur that inadvertently makes Nick's height a testament to someone else's cooking — her own warmth accidentally undermines the compliment
jokes.explanation: Classic Jean Weir — well-meaning, slightly obtuse logic delivered with complete sincerity. Her comfort-defensive maneuver of attributing Nick's height to nutrition is a wonderfully weird thought that only Jean would produce.

jokes.text: Nick reveals at dinner that he needs a sleeping bag because he's been 'spending a lot of time sleeping on people's floors lately'
jokes.setup: Nick pivots the conversation to sleeping bags, supposedly for camping
jokes.punchline: When pressed on why, Nick admits to his homeless-by-floor situation at a family dinner table — the casual overshare in this domestic context is the joke
jokes.explanation: The slow reveal of Nick's predicament to Harold at his own dinner table has a nice comic progression. Harold has gone from stranger-at-the-door to learning this kid is essentially couch-surfing in the span of one meal.

jokes.text: 'He said my drums were interfering with my schoolwork.' [beat] 'I think my schoolwork was interfering with my drums.'
jokes.setup: Nick explains why his dad sold his drums (interfering with school)
jokes.punchline: Nick inverts the priority order completely — his correction reveals he genuinely considers music primary and school an obstacle
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the show's best lines. The structure is flawless — mirrored syntax, single-word inversion, devastating character revelation. Nick isn't being ironic or defiant: he is genuinely correcting an error in logic. The pause before the correction makes it land harder. Harold's 'Terrible.' response (id 24) is the perfect button. Could only come from Nick Andopolis, could only matter in this moment. Quotable, structurally precise, character-defining.

jokes.text: Harold's response to Nick's drum philosophy: 'Terrible.'
jokes.setup: Nick delivers his inverted logic about schoolwork interfering with drums
jokes.punchline: Harold's single-word verdict — not angry, not laughing, just quietly appalled — is the perfect button
jokes.explanation: One word. Harold Weir in one word. This is a masterpiece of comedic economy — Harold processes an entire worldview incompatible with his own and produces 'Terrible.' It's contemptuous, funny, and completely Harold. Could justify an 8 because the economy is as good as it gets and it's perfectly timed as a button on joke 23.

jokes.text: Harold immediately offers Nick the couch after calling his worldview 'Terrible'
jokes.setup: Harold has just dismissed Nick's drum philosophy
jokes.punchline: Despite finding Nick's logic 'Terrible,' Harold's decency immediately overrides his judgment and he offers the couch — the joke is in the gap between Harold's critique and his generosity
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Harold's inability to resist helping someone in need even when he disapproves of them. His generosity undercuts his gruffness. This reveals Harold's character more fully through the absurdity of the pivot.

jokes.text: Nick: 'It's like you cooked this in heaven... then brought it back down here to your table.'
jokes.setup: Nick continues gushing over the pot roast
jokes.punchline: Nick's compliment reaches a theological register — describing the pot roast as a divine artifact brought earthward — which is both endearing and unhinged
jokes.explanation: Nick's hyperbolic neediness reaches its peak absurdity here. The escalation of his food-worship is funny because it's both sincere and completely disproportionate. The specific image of cooking in heaven and bringing it back is wonderfully odd.

jokes.text: Bill's pre-emptive alibi to Cindy: 'That wasn't me. That was Neal. He just said it was me.'
jokes.setup: Cindy approaches Bill wanting to talk; Bill immediately assumes he's in trouble
jokes.punchline: Before she can even say what she wants, Bill has already prepared a denial and a Neal-blaming cover story — which tells us both that Bill constantly does things requiring denial, and that this scheme is false
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. Bill's reflexive preemptive denial — immediately blaming Neal for something unspecified — is deeply funny because of what it implies about both his general conduct and his relationship with Neal. 'He just said it was me' is a hilariously specific detail for a denial about something we haven't established. A joke that earns laughs purely through character.

jokes.text: Cindy tells Bill she 'like-likes' Sam — Bill's stunned 'Oh. OH!'
jokes.setup: Cindy says she 'likes' Sam; Bill says he does too
jokes.punchline: Cindy clarifies she 'like-likes' Sam — the double-like usage is the universal teen-speak distinction, and Bill's double-take realization plays the moment perfectly
jokes.explanation: The 'like-like' construction is a well-worn teen convention, but Bill's staged realization — the delayed 'Oh. OH!' — sells it. Not original in concept but executed with Bill's specific awkward earnestness.

jokes.text: Bill: 'Me, too.' / Girl: 'No, I LIKE-like Sam.' / Bill: 'Oh.' ... 'Oh!'
jokes.setup: Girl tells Bill she 'likes' Sam. Bill takes it at face value.
jokes.punchline: Bill's double-take — the first 'Oh.' of comprehension, then the second 'Oh!' of full realization — played out in stages. His flat, delayed processing of the distinction between 'like' and 'like-like' is perfectly Bill.
jokes.explanation: The staggered 'Oh.' / 'Oh!' is a performance joke dependent on Bill's specific deadpan delivery — the gap between the two 'oh's is the joke. Pure character work; only Bill would process this that slowly. The like/like-like distinction is an eternal teen comedy beat but Bill's execution elevates it.

jokes.text: Kim to Lindsay: 'Did he try to give you a back rub?' / 'No.' / 'Well, he will.' / 'If he tries to give you a foot massage...run.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is worried about Nick being in her house.
jokes.punchline: Kim's escalating warning system — back rub (concern) → foot massage (flee immediately) — implies a graduated threat assessment based on personal experience.
jokes.explanation: Kim's escalating massage-warning is funny because it implies a detailed taxonomy of unwanted physical contact she's developed from experience. 'If he tries to give you a foot massage...run' is quotable. Economy and character integration are both high — this could only be Kim.

jokes.text: Neal and Bill watching Sam and Cindy smile at each other: 'They're smiling.' Neal immediately pivots: Cindy's friends include Vicky, 'Oh man, I love her.'
jokes.setup: The geeks watch Sam successfully ask Cindy to the party from across the hall
jokes.punchline: Bill and Neal's instant calculation that Sam's success is their access point to popular girls — previously dismissed as 'pod people' and 'a cult' — shows their flexible principles
jokes.explanation: 'Bring on the pods.' Neal's willingness to abandon all his snobbishness about the popular crowd the instant he sees personal benefit is perfect adolescent comedy. The 'pod people/cult' callback makes this funnier — he called them that himself minutes ago.

jokes.text: 'They are. But that's one cult I wouldn't mind joining. Bring on the pods.'
jokes.setup: Bill points out Neal previously called Cindy's friends a 'cult' and 'pod people'
jokes.punchline: Neal acknowledges the contradiction, then doubles down: 'Bring on the pods.' — complete ideological surrender in four words
jokes.explanation: 'Bring on the pods.' is a great line — four words that collapse all of Neal's earlier intellectual posturing. It's a callback that pays off economically and reveals character. Neal's complete lack of shame about the reversal makes it funnier. Strong candidate for the episode's most quotable moment outside joke 23.

jokes.text: Nick is playing air drums in the Weir living room at night — Harold appears and tells him it's 'quiet hour'
jokes.setup: Nick is a guest on the Weirs' couch
jokes.punchline: He is playing intense air drums to Rush's Tom Sawyer in someone else's living room in the middle of the night — the visual image of this enormous teenager drumming furiously in the Weir's quiet home is the joke
jokes.explanation: Visually strong — Nick playing air drums to Neil Peart while crashing in a stranger's house, completely unselfconscious. The 'quiet hour' concept applied to someone doing silent air drumming is an extra layer of absurdity.

jokes.text: Nick says Harold is 'starting to sound like my dad' — Harold: 'Well, then, your dad is a smart man.'
jokes.setup: Harold tells Nick to do his homework instead of air drumming
jokes.punchline: Nick deploys the teen nuclear option ('you sound like my dad') as a rebuke; Harold accepts the comparison as a compliment, refusing to be wounded
jokes.explanation: Harold's refusal to be offended by the teen insult — turning 'you sound like my dad' into praise for his parental wisdom — is a nice Harold moment. The kid aimed to wound; Harold neutralized it effortlessly.

jokes.text: Nick: 'You're starting to sound like my dad, Mr. Weir.' Harold: 'Oh, yeah? Well, then, your dad is a smart man.'
jokes.setup: Nick uses the 'you sound like my dad' line as a mild insult/complaint.
jokes.punchline: Harold doesn't take the bait — he embraces the comparison and defends Nick's dad simultaneously, which subverts the expected parental-solidarity lecture into a one-liner.
jokes.explanation: Harold is at his best when he's laconic and right. 'Well, then, your dad is a smart man' as a complete, dignified response to what was meant as a complaint is very Harold — self-assured, unflappable, one line. High character integration.

jokes.text: Harold on Neil Peart: 'Neil Peart couldn't drum his way out of a paper bag.'
jokes.setup: Nick is listening to Rush — Harold says the drummer is 'terrible'
jokes.punchline: Nick reveals it's Neil Peart, widely regarded as one of the greatest rock drummers alive; Harold is unimpressed and dismisses him with a classic idiom
jokes.explanation: Harold Weir dismissing Neil Peart is peak Harold. It's both wrong (Peart is universally beloved) and completely in character — Harold grew up with Krupa and Rich, so Peart sounds like noise. The line will resonate with anyone who knows Rush, and the confidence of his wrongness is funny. A great Harold-vs.-teenager-culture moment.

jokes.text: Nick: 'That's Neil Peart. He's the greatest drummer alive.' Harold: 'Neil Peart couldn't drum his way out of a paper bag.'
jokes.setup: Harold has called Nick's favorite drummer 'terrible'
jokes.punchline: Nick's defense of Peart vs. Harold's total refusal to back down despite being objectively wrong — the collision of two completely incompatible musical worldviews
jokes.explanation: The exchange as a unit — Harold's confident dismissal followed by Nick's outrage followed by Harold's doubled-down idiom — works as a comedic confrontation of generations. The twist is that Harold then gets Nick to listen to his old records and they bond over it, making this a thematic joke too.

jokes.text: Nick and Harold bonding over old jazz drumming records, Nick: 'That guy is amazing. How did you hear about him?' Harold: 'Are you kidding? I grew up with Gene Krupa and Buddy Rich.'
jokes.setup: Harold plays old jazz drumming as proof that Peart is overrated
jokes.punchline: Nick is genuinely blown away by the old records — and Harold's 'Are you kidding?' implies Nick should know these names, which Nick absolutely doesn't
jokes.explanation: The generational grudging respect scene played for gentle comedy. Harold's 'Are you kidding?' when Nick doesn't recognize Krupa is funny because Harold lives in a world where these names are obvious — and Nick lives in 1980 where they aren't.

jokes.text: Lindsay advising Sam on girls: 'Nick was so into me, he made me want to move to a different country.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is giving Sam relationship advice — be yourself, be a gentleman, don't smother her
jokes.punchline: Lindsay illustrates 'don't smother' with the Nick anecdote — Nick's romantic intensity was so overwhelming that international relocation seemed like the rational response
jokes.explanation: This line is legitimately great. The specificity — not 'a different city' or 'away from him' but 'a different COUNTRY' — is perfectly calibrated. It's both a devastating critique of Nick's romantic intensity and a darkly funny piece of relationship advice, delivered from the context of Nick currently camping in their living room. The irony that Nick is literally in the next room makes it funnier. High quotability, perfect economy, specific to Lindsay and Nick's history.

jokes.text: Lindsay advising Sam: 'Just be yourself... Be a gentleman and don't be weird and don't smother her.' Then adds: 'Nick was so into me, he made me want to move to a different country.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay gives Sam earnest advice about being with Cindy.
jokes.punchline: The advice pivots to a personal horror story — Nick's romantic intensity was so overwhelming it inspired international relocation fantasies. The geographic scale of her response is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the disproportionate scale — not 'uncomfortable' or 'suffocating' but literally moving countries. Lindsay's delivery as throwaway laughter makes it land. Also works as character comedy for Nick (offscreen confirmation of his intensity) and as relationship advice that accidentally undermines itself.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I don't think Nick's in love with you. I think he's in love with Mom and Dad.'
jokes.setup: Nick's ongoing excessive gratitude and devotion to the Weir household
jokes.punchline: Lindsay correctly identifies that Nick has transferred his romantic fixation from her to her parents — the absurdity of a teenage boy being in love with someone else's parents
jokes.explanation: A very sharp observational joke that is also emotionally true — Nick does love the Weirs as a family. The 'in love with Mom and Dad' framing is funny because it uses romantic vocabulary for a non-romantic situation. Lindsay's deadpan delivery of this insight is perfectly character-integrated — she's both amused and slightly weirded out. Top-tier episode moment.

jokes.text: Nick wakes Harold up at 2am whispering thanks through his bedroom door
jokes.setup: Nick is crashing in the Weir house
jokes.punchline: Nick wakes Harold up in the middle of the night to express gratitude — a gesture so socially ill-timed it circles back around to touching, but is primarily uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe-comedy. Nick's compulsive need to express gratitude even at 2am is consistent with his needy character. Harold's patient 'Go back to bed' / 'I know' / 'Good night' is funny in its restrained resignation.

jokes.text: Nick catches Sam in pajamas in the hallway: 'Those are, uh, nice pajamas.'
jokes.setup: Sam bumps into Nick while wandering to the bathroom at night
jokes.punchline: Nick's strained attempt at small talk — commenting on pajamas — highlights how completely weird the situation has become: Lindsay's ex-boyfriend is now sleeping in their house and encountering family members at night
jokes.explanation: Good awkward-silence comedy. The compliment on pajamas is what you say when you have absolutely nothing to say. Nick's visible effort to be normal in an abnormal situation is the joke.

jokes.text: Sam's confessional: 'This is a whole new world. I see double-dating and triple-dating and cheerleaders at your house and me at your house.'
jokes.setup: Sam has successfully asked Cindy to a party
jokes.punchline: Sam's vision of his romantic future becomes a snowballing fantasy of social ascension — from one date to a whole social ecosystem built around cheerleaders — the absurdity of the projection is the joke
jokes.explanation: Sam's exponential romantic fantasy is adolescent wish fulfillment taken to its logical extreme. The specificity of the escalation (double then triple dating, then cheerleaders at his house) is funny because Sam has done almost nothing yet.

jokes.text: Neal: 'She's the one that should be thankful. You're a catch.'
jokes.setup: Sam thanks Neal for the encouragement about Cindy.
jokes.punchline: Neal's assertion that Sam — a small, socially awkward 14-year-old geek — is a catch that Cindy should feel lucky to have is delivered with the unironic confidence of a man who also considers himself a catch.
jokes.explanation: Mild character comedy — Neal's delusional confidence in the group's desirability is a recurring trait. Functional but not the sharpest Neal moment.

jokes.text: Make-out party panic — Neal explains it's a make-out party, Sam didn't know, Neal: 'Well, you better get ready to make out.'
jokes.setup: Sam is going to a party with Cindy and is nervous about kissing
jokes.punchline: Neal reveals the party's specific purpose is making out — Sam's date just got significantly higher-stakes
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Sam's anxiety. The reveal of the party's purpose raises the stakes perfectly, and Neal's matter-of-fact delivery ('Well, you better get ready to make out') is characteristically blunt.

jokes.text: The French kissing debate — Bill's clinical disgust: 'Germs. Spit. Mucus. Old bits of food. That's just to name a couple.'
jokes.setup: The geeks debate whether Sam should French kiss Cindy
jokes.punchline: Bill launches into a medical opposition brief against French kissing — listing biological contaminants with clinical precision, then adding 'that's just to name a couple' as if there's a much longer list
jokes.explanation: 'That's just to name a couple' is the killer line — Bill's calm implication that his list of mouth-horrors is actually an abbreviated sample. This is a perfectly Bill joke: hypochondriac, clinical, delivered with total sincerity. The specificity of 'old bits of food' is grotesquely accurate. Strong character integration — no one else in this show would frame a kiss this way.

jokes.text: Bill escalates: 'Do you make your tongue hard or soft?' / 'Are you supposed to lick her teeth?'
jokes.setup: Bill has been warned off French kissing by his germ concerns
jokes.punchline: He continues with a series of technical kissing questions so specific and bizarre they're clearly the product of genuine anxious overthinking rather than mockery
jokes.explanation: Bill's technical interrogation of kissing mechanics is deeply funny because it's sincere — he actually needs to know. 'Hard or soft' and 'lick her teeth' are the kinds of questions only someone who has never kissed anyone and has thought about it way too much would ask. Pure Bill.

jokes.text: Bill's escalating vomit hypothetical: 'What if I throw up? What if I throw up all over her? What if I throw up IN her mouth?'
jokes.setup: Neal tells Sam how to French kiss; Bill raises his objections
jokes.punchline: Bill's anxiety spirals through three levels of vomit catastrophe — the third one ('in her mouth') is the peak of the escalation and genuinely revolting in a way that makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: Textbook escalation comedy with perfect structure. Three beats: on her, all over her, in her mouth. Each level is worse and funnier. 'In her mouth' is simultaneously the most disgusting and funniest possible endpoint, and Bill's genuine horror at the prospect sells it. This is a standout comedy moment — the structure is nearly perfect, the character integration is flawless (no one but Bill would think this), and the payoff lands hard.

jokes.text: Gordon: 'I'm saving my virginity for the future Mrs. Crisp.' Harris: 'I have a date with Judith. Every night's a make-out party with us. Whoopie.'
jokes.setup: Sam is trying to figure out who can come to the make-out party
jokes.punchline: Gordon's virginity announcement and Harris's completely mirthless 'Every night's a make-out party with us. Whoopie.' — both delivered as statements of fact that undermine themselves
jokes.explanation: Two quick jokes that both land. Gordon's virginity-saving speech is pompous self-delusion. Harris's 'Whoopie' is perfect deadpan — he has a girlfriend and is somehow conveying complete romantic exhaustion in one word. Both very specific supporting character moments.

jokes.text: Neal reveals he has been 'practicing' spinning a bottle all week and demonstrates controlled torque technique
jokes.setup: They're heading to a spin-the-bottle make-out party
jokes.punchline: Neal has apparently spent his week scientifically training to control a spin-the-bottle spin — and explains it with the vocabulary of physics ('torque,' 'revolutions,' 'percentage')
jokes.explanation: Peak Neal. He has engineered a solution to random chance in service of getting kissed, and he has done it with genuine scientific rigor. 'I've been practicing all week' followed by a physics explanation is deeply funny because it reveals Neal's mind — obsessive, systematic, and aimed at the most desperate possible goal. Classic geek comedy.

jokes.text: Bill: 'What if they don't wanna kiss us?' Neal: 'That's the genius part of the game. They have to!'
jokes.setup: Bill raises the concern that girls might not want to kiss them
jokes.punchline: Neal's triumphant 'They have to!' — the genius of spin-the-bottle as a consent-bypassing mechanism, delivered as though he has invented something revolutionary
jokes.explanation: Neal's revelation that the game's social contract is its appeal — girls must comply — is funny because he says it with such unironic delight. His joy at having found a socially sanctioned workaround to rejection is perfectly adolescent.

jokes.text: Bill: 'I just don't wanna see the expression on their face when the bottle lands on me.' Neal: 'Who cares if they look disappointed? All I care about is if they look disappointed after the kiss.'
jokes.setup: Bill is worried about girls' disappointment when they have to kiss him
jokes.punchline: Neal reframes the concern: pre-kiss disappointment is fine — the only question is whether he can change it. His confidence is equal parts tragic and funny.
jokes.explanation: Neal's reframe is both funny and oddly poignant — he's accepted that girls will be disappointed to kiss him, but he believes in his ability to change their minds. The confidence is delusional but weirdly admirable. Bill's specific fear ('the expression on their face') grounds it in real adolescent pain, making the joke richer. One of the episode's better Bill/Neal exchanges.

jokes.text: Nick and Daniel argue about turtlenecks: 'We'll look like the Smothers Brothers.'
jokes.setup: Both Nick and Daniel are wearing turtlenecks and realize they can't both wear them to the party
jokes.punchline: Daniel invokes the Smothers Brothers as the worst-case scenario of double-turtleneck aesthetics
jokes.explanation: The Smothers Brothers reference is period-appropriate and funny — the idea that a matching-turtleneck situation is only one step from a folk-comedy duo is nicely specific. The premise of the argument (two guys can't both wear turtlenecks) is inherently funny.

jokes.text: Daniel removes his turtleneck to reveal a dickey underneath — 'Nice dickey!' 'Shut up.'
jokes.setup: Daniel concedes and removes his turtleneck so Nick can wear his
jokes.punchline: Daniel has a dickey underneath — a fake turtleneck collar, the ultimate in aesthetic deception — and Nick immediately calls him on it
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of Daniel's dickey — a garment that embodies pretending to be cooler than you are — is perfect for a character who projects effortless cool while actually making deeply uncool choices. 'Shut up' as his only defense is delightfully deflated.

jokes.text: Bill comparing entering the party to the Animal House scene where Flounder gets put in a room with 'the blind guy and the Indian guy'
jokes.setup: Bill is scared to go into the popular kids' party
jokes.punchline: His cultural reference for social rejection is the most obscure possible scene from Animal House, and his reasoning — that being socially exiled is like Flounder's initiation — is both accurate and completely neurotic
jokes.explanation: Bill's anxiety translated into an Animal House metaphor is very Bill. The fact that he can summon a specific scene from a specific movie to describe a social situation shows his mind works through movies — a callback to joke 10.

jokes.text: Bill's philosophical reframe: 'Blind guys are cool. They have supersonic hearing.' / 'You're right. Let's mingle.'
jokes.setup: Bill has likened their social situation to the worst room in Animal House
jokes.punchline: Sam refuses to accept the framing and finds one positive — blind guys have supersonic hearing — and this genuinely resolves Bill's anxiety
jokes.explanation: 'Blind guys are cool. They have supersonic hearing.' / 'You're right. Let's mingle.' is a lovely quick comedic exchange. The logic is non-sequitur but Bill immediately accepts it. Sam and Bill's weird logic-based friendship communicates perfectly in this moment.

jokes.text: Nick at the Weir dinner table demonstrates his drum lesson: 'Paradiddle, paradiddle, paradiddle.'
jokes.setup: Nick excitedly reports on his drum lesson while the Weirs try to eat dinner
jokes.punchline: Nick demonstrates his new technique — air drumming at the dinner table, saying 'paradiddle' — with complete unselfconsciousness
jokes.explanation: Nick demonstrating his new skill at the dinner table with total obliviousness to social norms is consistent with his character. 'Paradiddle' is an intrinsically funny word to repeat three times.

jokes.text: Harold reveals he hired Nick as a stock boy to pay for drum lessons — without telling Lindsay
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks where Nick got money for drum lessons
jokes.punchline: It was Harold — the gruff, skeptical father has secretly arranged an employment scheme to get Nick drum lessons without anyone knowing
jokes.explanation: The reveal recontextualizes Harold's entire attitude toward Nick — 'Terrible' but also secretly arranging help. The joke is in the gap between Harold's crusty presentation and his actual behavior. Works both comedically and emotionally.

jokes.text: Spin the bottle — 'Why does the bottle keep landing on me?' vs. 'How come the bottle never lands on me?'
jokes.setup: Spin the bottle at the party — Neal has been practicing his technique all week
jokes.punchline: One girl is repeatedly getting the bottle and is sick of it; Neal is getting overlooked despite his week of preparation — the opposite of what both wanted
jokes.explanation: The payoff on Neal's spin-control scheme — it doesn't work — is funny because it deflates all that preparation. The additional irony that someone else is getting the bottle too often and complaining makes the cosmos of the joke complete.

jokes.text: A guy watching the bottle land on Bill three times: 'Hey, I'll give you 8 and a half minutes.'
jokes.setup: Bill keeps getting the bottle and has to do 7 minutes in heaven
jokes.punchline: A watching boy offers to upgrade Bill's heaven time — implying he expects Bill to be so bad at kissing that a girl would need more than 7 minutes of pep talk to recover
jokes.explanation: 'I'll give you 8 and a half' is a quick, sharp joke — the extra 90 seconds as consolation for having to kiss Bill. Specific and deadpan.

jokes.text: Nick's dad: 'You call me when he turns 16.' — after Harold's optimistic 'sometimes you just gotta let 'em be kids'
jokes.setup: Harold is gently suggesting that teenagers need room to be kids; Nick's dad has been harder on Nick
jokes.punchline: Nick's dad's 'call me when he turns 16' implies Harold's liberal parenting optimism will be tested, darkly suggesting things get worse — a knowing parent-to-parent warning
jokes.explanation: A short, devastating line delivered with absolute deadpan. Nick's dad has seen things. Harold is 14 years into optimism; Nick's dad is implying the real test is coming. The implication is both a dark joke (Harold has no idea what's coming with Lindsay) and a character reveal about how parental styles form in response to experience. One of the episode's best lines — economical, specific, and resonant.

jokes.text: 'There is obviously something wrong with that bottle.' — someone suggests switching seats to change Bill's luck
jokes.setup: The bottle keeps landing on Bill; it's disrupting the game
jokes.punchline: Someone articulates the absurd group belief that the bottle is defective — rather than accepting that the universe keeps selecting Bill
jokes.explanation: This is a stealth callback to The Jerk quote from the episode's opening — 'there's something wrong with these cans!' echoed as 'there is obviously something wrong with that bottle.' Whether intentional or not in the script, the parallel is delightful. Running gag conclusion.

jokes.text: After seat switch, the bottle still lands on Bill — 'NO!' [laughter] 'Just kiss him and get it over with.'
jokes.setup: They've changed seats to try to break Bill's bottle luck
jokes.punchline: The bottle finds Bill again anyway — the universe is specifically targeting him — followed by 'just kiss him and get it over with'
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the running gag reaches its comedic peak here — the intervention failed, the universe chose Bill again. 'Just kiss him and get it over with' is a great line for whoever delivers it: resigned, pragmatic, and slightly brutal.

jokes.text: Vicki and Bill sent into 7 minutes in heaven — Bill's terrified face vs. the crowd's 'VICKI AND BILL' chant
jokes.setup: For the third time the bottle lands on Bill — this time with Vicki, and 7 minutes in heaven is invoked
jokes.punchline: The crowd chants them in as Bill is essentially frog-marched into a closet — the public humiliation of the proceeding vs. whatever is happening inside
jokes.explanation: Vicki's 'Come on. Let's just get this over with.' is the comedic line — resigned acceptance, like going to the dentist. Bill's terror is the visual counterpoint.

jokes.text: Nick at the door: 'You sure you don't wanna adopt me?'
jokes.setup: Nick's dad has arrived to take him home; Nick is saying goodbye to the Weirs
jokes.punchline: Nick half-jokes about adoption — which is only slightly hyperbolic given how thoroughly he has embedded himself in their family
jokes.explanation: The adoption joke is the perfect button on the Nick-as-surrogate-Weir-kid plotline. After Lindsay's observation that Nick is in love with her parents, and Nick's own 'best family ever' outburst, the adoption request is both funny and emotionally true. Great closing beat for this story.

jokes.text: Sam and Cindy in a back room at the party — Sam defensively insisting he wasn't planning to kiss her; then Cindy pointing out he was trying to
jokes.setup: Sam has pulled Cindy into a private room at the party to talk — Cindy immediately assumes he'll try something
jokes.punchline: Sam's frantic denial ('You're not gonna touch me') colliding with what Sam was actually trying to do — leading to his accusation 'You're a jerk' and Cindy's 'I couldn't be less happy to be in here with you'
jokes.explanation: The transcript is garbled here making the exact comedy hard to read, but the situation — two teenagers alone in a room, both on guard, both wanting opposite things — is inherent cringe-comedy territory. The transcript corruption weakens assessment.

jokes.text: Sam: 'Well, here we are.' Cindy: 'Yeah. Here we are.' [long pause] Sam turns on the radio.
jokes.setup: Continued 7 minutes in heaven stalemate
jokes.punchline: The 'well here we are / yeah here we are' exchange is the perfect encapsulation of two people with nothing left to say — Sam's desperate radio-turning as a third person in the room
jokes.explanation: Six words ('Well, here we are. Yeah. Here we are.') capturing maximum awkwardness. The economy is excellent. The radio as social lifeline is a great physical comedy beat. This kind of quiet comedic embarrassment is the show at its best.

jokes.text: Bill asks Vicki in the closet: 'Do you think I'm pretty?' — Vicki: 'I don't know. This is the only way I've ever looked.'
jokes.setup: Bill and Vicki are alone in the closet for 7 minutes in heaven
jokes.punchline: Bill — having been objectified all night by an unwilling universe — turns the tables and asks a girl if she finds him pretty. Vicki's response is unexpectedly philosophical: she has no reference point for her own appearance.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. 'Do you think I'm pretty?' from Bill — the most unconfident geek at a make-out party — is already a sharp subversion. But Vicki's answer is the real comedy: 'I don't know. This is the only way I've ever looked.' It's a profoundly weird, philosophically sound, and unexpectedly sincere response that reframes Bill's question as unanswerable. Only Bill would ask it; only this moment would elicit that answer. It's funny and quietly touching. The originality is high, the character integration is perfect, and the structure (setup/non sequitur answer) is elegant. Standout moment.

jokes.text: Bill: 'They're never nice to me.' Vicki: 'Every time I look at you, you look like you're having a pretty good time. You're always laughing.' Bill: 'I watch movies in my head.'
jokes.setup: Bill reveals he feels unpretty and unloved; Vicki challenges this by pointing out he always seems happy
jokes.punchline: 'I watch movies in my head.' — Bill's explanation for his persistent happiness is that he runs a private internal cinema. It's both a perfect comedy line and a devastating character revelation.
jokes.explanation: This is the best line in the episode and one of the great character lines in the series. 'I watch movies in my head.' is five words that tell you everything about Bill Haverchuck: his rich inner life, his use of pop culture as emotional insulation, his solitary nature, and his gentleness. It's simultaneously a joke and a key that unlocks the character completely. The structure (Vicki's observation → Bill's impossible answer) is perfect. The originality is exceptional — no other character in television history has given this answer to this question. It's calibrated against the 9.5 anchor and holds up.

jokes.text: Harold: 'I'm sorry you think I don't treat you right sometimes.' Lindsay: 'I didn't say that, Dad.'
jokes.setup: Harold is having a rare moment of emotional vulnerability with Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Harold preemptively apologizes for a criticism Lindsay didn't actually make — his over-defensive self-awareness is the joke
jokes.explanation: Light comedy in an emotional scene. Harold's reflexive defensive apology is a classic Dad move — funnier for how small it is in a larger tender moment.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Dad, I can't stay inside all the time.' Harold: 'I know.' [pause] 'Why not?'
jokes.setup: Harold has confessed that every second Lindsay is out of the house is torture for him
jokes.punchline: 'I know. Why not?' — Harold acknowledges he knows she can't stay inside and then immediately asks why — the dad-joke that is also genuine
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly constructed laugh in the middle of a genuinely tender scene. Harold says 'I know' — the reasonable, adult answer — then immediately undercuts it with 'Why not?' which is both a joke and reveals his actual wish. The pause between them is the comedy. It's a perfect Harold Weir moment: self-aware enough to know the right answer, desperate enough to ask the other one anyway. Lindsay laughing at this is exactly right. One of the episode's best Harold moments.

jokes.text: The geeks' closing scene: someone re-narrates The Jerk cans scene for Sam and they both laugh
jokes.setup: Episode bookend — the opening had the geeks quoting The Jerk with delight
jokes.punchline: The episode closes on the geeks still finding The Jerk funny — the callback completes the frame and suggests that for these boys, a good movie quote never expires
jokes.explanation: Solid structural callback that gives the episode a thematic bow — while everything else changed (Nick's situation, Sam's romantic life, Lindsay's relationship with Harold), the geeks still find The Jerk just as funny. There's something quietly beautiful about that, and the laugh it earns is earned.

jokes.text: Sam and Cindy's first kiss is interrupted: 'Hold on one second.' — Bill and Vicki's 7 minutes are long over: 'Bill, your 7 minutes were up 5 minutes ago. Vicki, your nightmare's over.'
jokes.setup: Sam has finally worked up to asking Cindy if he can kiss her — she says yes — then the door is immediately knocked on
jokes.punchline: Bill and Vicki have significantly overrun their 7 minutes, and someone cheerfully announces 'Vicki, your nightmare's over.' — The interruption of Sam's romantic moment plus the labeling of Bill's time in heaven as Vicki's 'nightmare' is the comedy
jokes.explanation: 'Vicki, your nightmare's over.' is the funniest line in the spin-the-bottle subplot — the casual cruelty of it, the fact that it's probably true, and that it interrupts Sam's actual romantic moment makes it land doubly. Excellent timing and structure.

jokes.text: Bill emerging from the closet: 'God, this really sucks.' — then door knocking, 'Bill. Bill. Hello, Bill.'
jokes.setup: Bill has just emerged from an extended 7 minutes in heaven
jokes.punchline: Bill's verdict on the experience is a flat 'God, this really sucks' — whatever happened in that closet, Bill is unimpressed. The repeated 'Bill. Bill. Hello, Bill.' knocking implies he's still in there, processing.
jokes.explanation: The ending of Bill's arc in this episode — whatever happened in there was apparently bad, or at least deeply unsatisfying, given that Bill's entire night was a disaster from the bottle's first landing. 'God, this really sucks' as a reaction to one's first extended alone-time with a girl is very Bill — sincere, deflated, unglamorous.

tmdb_overview: Kim's friend, Karen, bullies Sam and calls him a geek. Kim invites Lindsay to dinner at her house, exposing Lindsay to the abuse Kim takes at home.
